text: The power of speech is the chief distinction between humans and animals, and the most successful means of achieving human cooperation.
context: Foundational claim for the value of rhetorical education.

text: We should not call those men happy who are proud and overbearing, but those who, while possessing the greatest goods, think modestly of themselves.
context: Advocating moral leadership among the Greeks.

text: The true test of education is not what a man knows, but what he does with his knowledge and how he uses his speech for the good of the city.
context: Arguing that rhetoric must serve ethics and the polis.

text: It is not the man who has the most gold who is the richest, but the man who has the fewest wants and is most content with what he has.
speech: To Nicocles (letter of advice to a ruler)
context: Advice on the character required of a good prince.

text: Those who are capable of the greatest good are also capable of the greatest harm; therefore the training of the mind is the most important of all studies.
context: Warning about the double-edged power of eloquence.

text: We must not judge a man by his words alone, but by whether his actions are in harmony with his speech.
context: Insisting on integrity between logos and ergon.

text: The most excellent thing is to be able to speak well and to persuade; the second is to be able to listen well and to judge fairly.
context: Balancing the arts of speaking and critical listening.

text: A city is not made great by its walls or its harbors, but by the wisdom and virtue of its citizens and the quality of its leaders.
context: Calling for Greek unity under moral and intellectual leadership.

text: He who is a good counselor to his friends and a wise guide to his city deserves greater honor than the man who wins many battles.
context: Elevating the orator-statesman over the mere general.

text: We should strive to make our words such that they will be remembered not for their beauty alone, but for the good they do in the world.
context: Purpose of rhetorical art.

text: The man who can speak most eloquently on behalf of justice will be the true benefactor of his fellow citizens.
context: Linking eloquence directly to the defense of justice.

text: Education is the best provision for old age; it furnishes a man with a treasure that time cannot take away.
context: Long-term value of paideia (cultivation).

text: It is a noble thing to be the teacher of those who will one day lead their cities; it is a still nobler thing to be the teacher of those who will teach others.
context: Defense of his own profession as a rhetorician and educator.

text: The greatest glory is not in never falling, but in rising every time we fall and learning from the experience.
context: Practical wisdom for a young ruler.

text: We must not allow the desire for immediate advantage to lead us into actions that will bring lasting disgrace.
context: Long view in political decision-making.

text: Speech is the ruler and king of all things; with it we can make the false appear true and the true appear false.
context: Acknowledging the immense power (and responsibility) of logos.

text: A good orator must first be a good man; no amount of technical skill can compensate for a corrupt character.
context: The famous Isocratean ideal that character precedes technique.

text: Those who have the power to persuade have in their hands the greatest instrument for both good and evil.
context: The moral burden placed on the skilled speaker.

text: We should honor not those who can speak most cleverly, but those whose words are guided by justice and truth.
context: Rejection of sophistry in favor of ethical rhetoric.

text: The man who has learned to govern himself is fit to govern others; the man who cannot control his own tongue is fit for nothing.
context: Self-mastery as prerequisite for leadership.
